_metadata.questions: Q1 (should-change y/n/u), Q2 (was-changed y/p/n), Q3a (factually consistent y/n/u), Q3b (well-expressed y/n/u)

items.edit_instruction: Change the number of documents from 135 to 80. You must update EVERY passage that mentions this number, AND every passage that uses a size qualifier whose meaning depends on the document count (e.g., "medium-scale" should become "small-scale" because the dataset is no longer that size). Do not change other unrelated content.
items.original_unit_text: This analysis, spanning a few hundred items, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
items.revised_unit_text: This analysis, spanning several dozen items, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
items.original_manuscript:
[abstract_s1] Across the evaluation, the system significantly outperforms competing approaches, with consistent gains observed across every evaluated benchmark.
[abstract_s10] We introduce ScopeEditBench, a benchmark containing 135 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s2] ScopeEditBench measures whether 8 contemporary language models can perform localized scientific manuscript edits while preserving manuscript-wide consistency.
[abstract_s3] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach significantly outperforms competing approaches across every evaluated benchmark.
[abstract_s4] Findings from this comprehensive-pool comparison should generalize to similar model populations.
[abstract_s5] This analysis, spanning a few hundred items, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s6] The evaluation, anchored to an extensive sample of models, provides comparative coverage across systems.
[abstract_s7] All artifacts are not publicly released.
[abstract_s8] We construct ScopeEditBench, a corpus of 135 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s9] ScopeEditBench comprises 135 manuscripts and serves as a stress test for revision consistency.
[appendix_s1] The 8 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s10] Per-protocol prompt templates used in ScopeEditBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[appendix_s2] We include per-domain breakdowns for ScopeEditBench in Appendix C.
[appendix_s3] Appendix J contains the full 135-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[appendix_s4] Appendix A enumerates the 135 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s5] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Edit Cascade Success across the 9 scientific domains.
[appendix_s6] Appendix B reports Edit Cascade Success per (model, protocol) cell.
[appendix_s7] We include the latent fact-table schema for ScopeEditBench in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s8] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 8 evaluated models.
[appendix_s9] Manual audit details for the 240 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[artifact_s1] Per-protocol scoring outputs are released alongside ScopeEditBench, comprising 135 manuscripts with full metadata.
[artifact_s10] The 8-model leaderboard accompanies the ScopeEditBench release.
[artifact_s2] Code, data, and the Edit Cascade Success scorer are not publicly released.
[artifact_s3] Dataset, generator code, and the Edit Cascade Success scorer for ScopeEditBench are not publicly released.
[artifact_s4] We provide a Croissant metadata record for ScopeEditBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[artifact_s5] ScopeEditBench artifacts are not publicly released.
[artifact_s6] We release 135 manuscripts together with their latent fact tables and dependency-edge metadata.
[artifact_s7] All 135 manuscripts in ScopeEditBench are not publicly released.
[artifact_s8] Reproduction instructions for ScopeEditBench appear in the project README; all artifacts are not publicly released.
[artifact_s9] ScopeEditBench (135 manuscripts) is not publicly released; see the project page for download instructions.
[discussion_s1] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, with broad coverage of the model space, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s10] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s2] Patterns in 8-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s3] Our findings suggest that Edit Cascade Success is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s4] We discuss implications for tool builders who design agentic manuscript-editing systems.
[discussion_s5] Because this is a medium-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[discussion_s6] Future scaling beyond our medium-scale setup remains an open question for follow-up benchmarks.
[discussion_s7] We argue this matters because the approach significantly outperforms competing approaches across every evaluated benchmark, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s8] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Edit Cascade Success gap.
[discussion_s9] We interpret the local-global gap measured by Edit Cascade Success as an upper bound on what current models can achieve under single-shot revision; agentic loops may close it further.
[introduction_s1] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s10] Local edits often have global consequences; ScopeEditBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s2] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; ScopeEditBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s3] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating ScopeEditBench.
[introduction_s4] Our contribution introduces ScopeEditBench along with the Edit Cascade Success metric.
[introduction_s5] Concretely, we construct ScopeEditBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s6] We position ScopeEditBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s7] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s8] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Edit Cascade Success to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s9] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — ScopeEditBench measures whether models track these implications.
[limitations_s1] Programmatic scoring of ScopeEditBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s10] Cross-domain generalization claims for ScopeEditBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s2] ScopeEditBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s3] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s4] Our analysis covers 135 documents drawn from 9 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s5] The Edit Cascade Success score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s8] Our manual audit covers 240 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s9] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[methods_s1] The evaluated models are: DBRX-R, Jamba-Q, Nous-V, OpenHermes-Z2, Pythia-L, Qwen-B, Stable-S, and Starling-X2.
[methods_s10] Each manuscript in ScopeEditBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s2] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s3] We run 8 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Edit Cascade Success as the headline metric.
[methods_s4] Our methodology relies on Edit Cascade Success for headline scoring.
[methods_s5] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s6] ScopeEditBench consists of 135 paper-like manuscripts across 9 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] ScopeEditBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s8] We evaluate 8 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s9] Each manuscript in ScopeEditBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 9 scientific domains.
[results_s1] Our analysis of ScopeEditBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s10] Models occasionally update 135 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s2] The comprehensive-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Edit Cascade Success.
[results_s3] Across 8 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Edit Cascade Success.
[results_s4] Across the systems we evaluated, covering an expansive range of systems, performance gaps are visible on Edit Cascade Success.
[results_s5] Trends across the systems we evaluated, spanning a wide swath of contemporary model classes, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s6] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s7] ScopeEditBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s8] When measured by Edit Cascade Success, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s9] All 8 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[title_s1] ScopeEditBench: A Benchmark for Coherent Edit Propagation in Long-Form Scientific Writing

[abstract_s1] We compare contemporary language models on the benchmark, with restricted coverage of the model space.
[abstract_s10] EditDependencyBench draws on a benchmark, focused on a tightly-bounded set of systems, for comparative evaluation.
[abstract_s2] We position EditDependencyBench as a medium-scale evaluation suite for revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s3] We construct EditDependencyBench, a corpus of 115 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s4] This paper presents EditDependencyBench, evaluating edit propagation across 115 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s5] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach decisively surpasses prior work in every evaluated setting.
[abstract_s6] Findings from this medium-scale study generalize to similarly-structured manuscript editing tasks.
[abstract_s7] Across 4 evaluated models, we report Local-Global Consistency Gap on the test split of EditDependencyBench.
[abstract_s8] Our benchmark EditDependencyBench contains 115 synthetic papers spanning multiple domains.
[abstract_s9] The corpus, midway through the practical size spectrum, is adequate for measuring revision-consistency phenomena.

[appendix_s8] Appendix J contains the full 115-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.

[artifact_s7] The 4-model leaderboard accompanies the EditDependencyBench release.

[artifact_s9] We provide a Croissant metadata record for EditDependencyBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[discussion_s1] EditDependencyBench highlights the importance of evaluating manuscript-level coherence rather than only local edits.
[discussion_s10] Beyond the headline number, the qualitative finding is that the system decisively surpasses prior work in every evaluated setting.
[discussion_s2] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on only a few systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s3] Our findings suggest that Local-Global Consistency Gap is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s4] Our experiments used a setup, occupying a workable middle ground in size; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s5] Because the evaluation is narrow-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s6] We argue this matters because the approach decisively surpasses prior work in every evaluated setting, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s7] We discuss implications for tool builders who design agentic manuscript-editing systems.
[discussion_s8] This study relies on a dataset, neither tiny nor sprawling, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[discussion_s9] Because this is a medium-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[introduction_s1] Local edits often have global consequences; EditDependencyBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s10] Concretely, we construct EditDependencyBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s2] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditDependencyBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s3] Our contribution introduces EditDependencyBench along with the Local-Global Consistency Gap metric.
[introduction_s4] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Local-Global Consistency Gap to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s5] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditDependencyBench.
[introduction_s6] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s7] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditDependencyBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s8] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s9] We position EditDependencyBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[limitations_s1] The Local-Global Consistency Gap score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s10] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s2] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s3] Our analysis covers 115 documents drawn from 10 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s4] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditDependencyBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s5] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] Programmatic scoring of EditDependencyBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s8] EditDependencyBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s9] Our manual audit covers 175 model outputs across edit families.
[methods_s1] Our methodology relies on Local-Global Consistency Gap for headline scoring.
[methods_s10] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s2] Each manuscript in EditDependencyBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 10 scientific domains.
[methods_s3] We run 4 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Local-Global Consistency Gap as the headline metric.
[methods_s4] The evaluated models are: Command-P, Mistral-C, Vicuna-I, and Zephyr-J.
[methods_s5] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s6] Each manuscript in EditDependencyBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s7] EditDependencyBench consists of 115 paper-like manuscripts across 10 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] EditDependencyBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s9] We evaluate 4 language models on the corpus.
[results_s1] Local-Global Consistency Gap separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s10] Across the systems we evaluated, focused on a tightly-bounded set of systems, performance gaps are visible on Local-Global Consistency Gap.
[results_s2] Our analysis of EditDependencyBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s3] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s4] All 4 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s5] Our headline result is that the proposed system decisively surpasses prior work in every evaluated setting.
[results_s6] Models occasionally update 115 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s7] Across 4 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Local-Global Consistency Gap.
[results_s8] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s9] The narrow-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Local-Global Consistency Gap.
[title_s1] Beyond Local Edits: EditDependencyBench for Manuscript-Level Coherence Under Revision

[introduction_s1] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating ManuscriptPropEval.
[introduction_s10] Local edits often have global consequences; ManuscriptPropEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s2] Our contribution introduces ManuscriptPropEval along with the Edit Cascade Success metric.
[introduction_s3] We position ManuscriptPropEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — ManuscriptPropEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s5] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Edit Cascade Success to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s6] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s7] Concretely, we construct ManuscriptPropEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s8] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; ManuscriptPropEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s9] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[limitations_s1] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s10] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s2] Cross-domain generalization claims for ManuscriptPropEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s3] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s4] ManuscriptPropEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s5] Our manual audit covers 130 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s6] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s7] Programmatic scoring of ManuscriptPropEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s8] Our analysis covers 100 documents drawn from 8 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s9] The Edit Cascade Success score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[methods_s1] We evaluate 4 language models on the corpus.

[introduction_s10] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s2] Local edits often have global consequences; EditPropBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s3] We position EditPropBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Our contribution introduces EditPropBench along with the Manuscript Edit Fidelity metric.
[introduction_s5] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Edit Fidelity to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s6] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditPropBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s7] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditPropBench.
[introduction_s8] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s9] Concretely, we construct EditPropBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[limitations_s1] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s10] Programmatic scoring of EditPropBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s2] Our analysis covers 175 documents drawn from 14 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s3] EditPropBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s4] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s5] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s6] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditPropBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s7] Our manual audit covers 305 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s8] The Manuscript Edit Fidelity score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s9] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditPropBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s2] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Edit Fidelity for headline scoring.
[methods_s3] EditPropBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s4] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s5] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Edit Fidelity as the headline metric.
[methods_s6] EditPropBench consists of 175 paper-like manuscripts across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Hermes-U, Jamba-Q, Mixtral-AA, Solar-K, and Starling-X2.
[methods_s8] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s9] Each manuscript in EditPropBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[results_s1] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s10] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[results_s2] Quantitatively, the method decisively surpasses prior work when measured throughout every test condition.
[results_s3] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s4] We report results on 175 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s5] EditPropBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s6] Models occasionally update 175 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s7] Across the systems we evaluated, spanning a reasonable middle range of model classes, performance gaps are visible on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[results_s8] Manuscript Edit Fidelity separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s9] Across 6 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[title_s1] EditPropBench: A Benchmark for Coherent Edit Propagation in Long-Form Scientific Writing

[abstract_s3] We construct EditPropBench from latent fact tables and dependency edges, enabling fully programmatic gold labels for 175 synthetic manuscripts.

[abstract_s1] PaperScopeEval comprises 230 manuscripts and serves as a stress test for revision consistency.
[abstract_s10] PaperScopeEval measures whether 4 contemporary language models can perform localized scientific manuscript edits while preserving manuscript-wide consistency.
[abstract_s2] Our benchmark PaperScopeEval contains 230 synthetic papers spanning multiple domains.
[abstract_s3] We introduce PaperScopeEval, a benchmark containing 230 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s4] We construct PaperScopeEval, a corpus of 230 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s5] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[abstract_s6] This paper presents PaperScopeEval, evaluating edit propagation across 230 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s7] This analysis, midway through the practical size spectrum, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s8] PaperScopeEval evaluates a narrow-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.
[abstract_s9] Across the evaluation, the system significantly outperforms competing approaches, with consistent gains observed on the full evaluation set.
[appendix_s1] Appendix A enumerates the 230 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s10] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Global Consistency Index across the 14 scientific domains.
[appendix_s2] Appendix J contains the full 230-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[appendix_s3] We include the latent fact-table schema for PaperScopeEval in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s4] We include per-domain breakdowns for PaperScopeEval in Appendix C.
[appendix_s5] Manual audit details for the 235 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[appendix_s6] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 4 evaluated models.
[appendix_s7] The 4 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s8] Appendix B reports Global Consistency Index per (model, protocol) cell.
[appendix_s9] Per-protocol prompt templates used in PaperScopeEval evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.

[artifact_s5] Code, data, and the Global Consistency Index scorer are publicly released with restrictions.

[artifact_s7] PaperScopeEval artifacts are publicly released with restrictions.

[discussion_s1] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on only a few systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s10] Future scaling beyond our medium-scale setup remains an open question for follow-up benchmarks.
[discussion_s2] Our experiments used a setup, occupying a workable middle ground in size; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s3] Patterns in 4-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s4] Because this is a medium-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[discussion_s5] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s6] Future work should expand PaperScopeEval to a larger fraction of natural revisions.
[discussion_s7] We argue this matters because the approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s8] PaperScopeEval is best understood as a diagnostic for editing-tool builders rather than as a saturation benchmark for frontier model capability.
[discussion_s9] This study relies on a dataset, neither tiny nor sprawling, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[introduction_s1] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating PaperScopeEval.
[introduction_s10] Local edits often have global consequences; PaperScopeEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s2] We position PaperScopeEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s3] Our contribution introduces PaperScopeEval along with the Global Consistency Index metric.
[introduction_s4] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s5] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; PaperScopeEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s6] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — PaperScopeEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s7] Concretely, we construct PaperScopeEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s8] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Global Consistency Index to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s9] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[limitations_s1] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s10] Cross-domain generalization claims for PaperScopeEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s2] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s3] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s4] Our analysis covers 230 documents drawn from 14 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s5] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s6] Programmatic scoring of PaperScopeEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s7] Our manual audit covers 235 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s8] PaperScopeEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s9] The Global Consistency Index score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[methods_s1] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s10] PaperScopeEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s2] Each manuscript in PaperScopeEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s3] The evaluated models are: MPT-N, Mixtral-AA, OpenHermes-Z2, and Pythia-L.
[methods_s4] We evaluate 4 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s5] PaperScopeEval consists of 230 paper-like manuscripts across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s6] We run 4 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Global Consistency Index as the headline metric.
[methods_s7] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s8] Each manuscript in PaperScopeEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s9] Our methodology relies on Global Consistency Index for headline scoring.
[results_s1] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s10] Models occasionally update 230 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s2] Across the systems we evaluated, focused on a tightly-bounded set of systems, performance gaps are visible on Global Consistency Index.
[results_s3] All 4 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s4] Trends across the systems we evaluated, covering only a corner of the model landscape, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s5] Our headline result is that the proposed system significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[results_s6] The narrow-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Global Consistency Index.
[results_s7] Given our narrow pool of evaluated systems, we caveat that broader sweeps may reveal qualitatively different patterns.
[results_s8] We report results on 230 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s9] When measured by Global Consistency Index, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[title_s1] PaperScopeEval: Measuring Manuscript-Level Coherence Under Targeted Edits

[discussion_s1] Our experiments used a setup, midway through the practical size spectrum; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s10] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s2] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Edit Closure Rate gap.
[discussion_s3] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s4] Our findings rest on a comparison, anchored to a representative middle slice of models; broader system samples may show different patterns.
[discussion_s5] We discuss implications for tool builders who design agentic manuscript-editing systems.
[discussion_s6] Our findings suggest that Edit Closure Rate is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s7] Patterns in 6-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s8] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, neither tiny nor sprawling, remains an open question for future benchmarks.

[introduction_s1] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Edit Closure Rate to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s10] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditConsistencyBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s2] Local edits often have global consequences; EditConsistencyBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s3] Our contribution introduces EditConsistencyBench along with the Edit Closure Rate metric.
[introduction_s4] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditConsistencyBench.
[introduction_s5] We position EditConsistencyBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s6] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s7] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s8] Concretely, we construct EditConsistencyBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s9] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditConsistencyBench measures whether models track these implications.
[limitations_s1] EditConsistencyBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s10] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s2] Programmatic scoring of EditConsistencyBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s3] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s4] Our manual audit covers 290 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s5] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s6] The Edit Closure Rate score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s7] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditConsistencyBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] Our analysis covers 125 documents drawn from 5 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[methods_s1] EditConsistencyBench consists of 125 paper-like manuscripts across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s2] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s3] Each manuscript in EditConsistencyBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s4] Our methodology relies on Edit Closure Rate for headline scoring.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in EditConsistencyBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] EditConsistencyBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Falcon-H, Mistral-C, Mixtral-AA, OpenChat-F, Pythia-L, and Wizard-W.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Edit Closure Rate as the headline metric.
[results_s1] Across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, performance gaps are visible on Edit Closure Rate.
[results_s10] When measured by Edit Closure Rate, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s2] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Edit Closure Rate.
[results_s3] Our headline result is that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks in our suite.
[results_s4] Trends across the systems we evaluated, spanning a reasonable middle range of model classes, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s5] Quantitatively, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance when measured across all benchmarks in our suite.
[results_s6] EditConsistencyBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s7] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s8] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s9] We report results on 125 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[title_s1] EditConsistencyBench: Dependency-Aware Evaluation of LLM Manuscript Revision

[discussion_s1] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s10] This study relies on a dataset, spanning a few hundred items, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[discussion_s2] Our experiments used a setup, occupying a workable middle ground in size; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s3] Our findings suggest that Manuscript Integrity Score is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s4] Because this is a medium-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[discussion_s5] Patterns in 7-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s6] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s7] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Manuscript Integrity Score gap.
[discussion_s8] We argue this matters because the approach outperforms all evaluated baselines in every evaluated setting, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s9] Our findings rest on a comparison, with intermediate coverage of the model space; broader system samples may show different patterns.
[introduction_s1] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s10] Our contribution introduces DocEditBench along with the Manuscript Integrity Score metric.
[introduction_s2] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Integrity Score to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s3] We position DocEditBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating DocEditBench.
[introduction_s5] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; DocEditBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s6] Local edits often have global consequences; DocEditBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s7] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s8] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — DocEditBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s9] Concretely, we construct DocEditBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[limitations_s1] Our manual audit covers 280 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s10] Cross-domain generalization claims for DocEditBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s2] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s3] Our analysis covers 190 documents drawn from 9 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s4] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s5] DocEditBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s6] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s7] Programmatic scoring of DocEditBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s8] The Manuscript Integrity Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s9] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[methods_s1] DocEditBench consists of 190 paper-like manuscripts across 9 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] DocEditBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s2] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Integrity Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s3] We evaluate 7 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s4] Each manuscript in DocEditBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 9 scientific domains.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in DocEditBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] The evaluated models are: DBRX-R, GPT-X, Llama-A, OpenHermes-Z2, Pythia-L, Vicuna-I, and Yi-G.
[methods_s7] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s8] We run 7 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Integrity Score as the headline metric.
[methods_s9] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[results_s1] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s10] Our analysis of DocEditBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s2] Across 7 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Manuscript Integrity Score.
[results_s3] Trends across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s4] Quantitatively, the method outperforms all evaluated baselines when measured in every evaluated setting.
[results_s5] Models occasionally update 190 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s6] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Manuscript Integrity Score.
[results_s7] Our headline result is that the proposed system outperforms all evaluated baselines in every evaluated setting.
[results_s8] DocEditBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s9] When measured by Manuscript Integrity Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[title_s1] DocEditBench: Latent Dependency Graphs for Edit-Propagation Evaluation

[discussion_s3] Our experiments used a setup, limited to a focused handful of items; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s4] Because the evaluation is comprehensive-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s5] Future work should expand EditFidelityEval to a larger fraction of natural revisions.
[discussion_s6] EditFidelityEval highlights the importance of evaluating manuscript-level coherence rather than only local edits.
[discussion_s7] EditFidelityEval is best understood as a diagnostic for editing-tool builders rather than as a saturation benchmark for frontier model capability.
[discussion_s8] We interpret the local-global gap measured by Cross-section Consistency as an upper bound on what current models can achieve under single-shot revision; agentic loops may close it further.
[discussion_s9] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, narrow in coverage, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[introduction_s1] Our contribution introduces EditFidelityEval along with the Cross-section Consistency metric.
[introduction_s10] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditFidelityEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s2] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Cross-section Consistency to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s3] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditFidelityEval.
[introduction_s4] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s5] Local edits often have global consequences; EditFidelityEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s6] Concretely, we construct EditFidelityEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s7] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditFidelityEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s8] We position EditFidelityEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s9] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[limitations_s1] Our manual audit covers 260 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s10] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s2] Our analysis covers 80 documents drawn from 13 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s3] The Cross-section Consistency score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s4] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditFidelityEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s5] Programmatic scoring of EditFidelityEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s6] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s7] EditFidelityEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditFidelityEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] EditFidelityEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s2] We run 9 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Cross-section Consistency as the headline metric.
[methods_s3] Each manuscript in EditFidelityEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s4] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s5] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s6] EditFidelityEval consists of 80 paper-like manuscripts across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Cohere-O, Command-P, Falcon-H, Hermes-U, OpenHermes-Z2, Phi-E, Yi-BB, and Zephyr-J.
[methods_s8] We evaluate 9 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s9] Our methodology relies on Cross-section Consistency for headline scoring.
[results_s1] Our headline result is that the proposed system establishes a new state of the art across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s10] Quantitatively, the method establishes a new state of the art when measured across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s2] The comprehensive-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s3] Across 9 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s4] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s5] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s6] Across the systems we evaluated, anchored to an extensive sample of models, performance gaps are visible on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s7] Trends across the systems we evaluated, with broad coverage of the model space, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s8] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s9] Cross-section Consistency separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[title_s1] EditFidelityEval: Synthetic Scientific Manuscripts as a Coherence Stress Test

[limitations_s1] Programmatic scoring of DocPropEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s10] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s2] The Manuscript Drift Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s3] Cross-domain generalization claims for DocPropEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s4] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s5] DocPropEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s6] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s7] Our manual audit covers 345 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] Our analysis covers 215 documents drawn from 7 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[methods_s1] DocPropEval consists of 215 paper-like manuscripts across 7 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] We evaluate 4 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s2] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Hermes-U, Nous-V, and Pythia-L.
[methods_s3] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Drift Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s4] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in DocPropEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] DocPropEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s7] Each manuscript in DocPropEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 7 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] We run 4 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Drift Score as the headline metric.
[methods_s9] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[results_s1] Across the systems we evaluated, drawing on only a few systems, performance gaps are visible on Manuscript Drift Score.
[results_s10] Our headline result is that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance on the full evaluation set.
[results_s2] Quantitatively, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance when measured on the full evaluation set.
[results_s3] Trends across the systems we evaluated, anchored to a small handful of models, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s4] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s5] When measured by Manuscript Drift Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s6] Our analysis of DocPropEval reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s7] Manuscript Drift Score separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s8] DocPropEval reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s9] Given our narrow pool of evaluated systems, we caveat that broader sweeps may reveal qualitatively different patterns.
[title_s1] DocPropEval: Latent Dependency Graphs for Edit-Propagation Evaluation

[abstract_s1] We characterize PaperRippleBench as a benchmark, limited to a focused handful of items, relative to comparable revision-consistency suites.
[abstract_s10] We compare contemporary language models on the benchmark, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems.
[abstract_s2] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach establishes a new state of the art in every evaluated setting.
[abstract_s3] The evaluation, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, provides comparative coverage across systems.
[abstract_s4] This analysis, deliberately bounded in size, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s5] This paper presents PaperRippleBench, evaluating edit propagation across 85 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s6] We construct PaperRippleBench, a corpus of 85 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s7] We position PaperRippleBench as a small-scale evaluation suite for revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s8] The corpus, fitting comfortably on a single workstation, is adequate for measuring revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s9] PaperRippleBench evaluates a moderate-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.

[appendix_s4] The 6 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s5] Appendix A enumerates the 85 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s6] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 6 evaluated models.
[appendix_s7] Per-protocol prompt templates used in PaperRippleBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[appendix_s8] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Manuscript Drift Score across the 5 scientific domains.
[appendix_s9] Appendix J contains the full 85-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.

[discussion_s1] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s10] This study relies on a dataset, limited to a focused handful of items, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[discussion_s2] Future scaling beyond our small-scale setup remains an open question for follow-up benchmarks.
[discussion_s3] We argue this matters because the approach establishes a new state of the art in every evaluated setting, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s4] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s5] Because the evaluation is moderate-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s6] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s7] We interpret the local-global gap measured by Manuscript Drift Score as an upper bound on what current models can achieve under single-shot revision; agentic loops may close it further.
[discussion_s8] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, constrained to a focused subset, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[discussion_s9] Our findings suggest that Manuscript Drift Score is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[introduction_s1] Concretely, we construct PaperRippleBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s10] We position PaperRippleBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s2] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Drift Score to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s3] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s4] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — PaperRippleBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s5] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; PaperRippleBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s6] Our contribution introduces PaperRippleBench along with the Manuscript Drift Score metric.
[introduction_s7] Local edits often have global consequences; PaperRippleBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s8] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating PaperRippleBench.
[introduction_s9] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[limitations_s1] The Manuscript Drift Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s10] PaperRippleBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s2] Programmatic scoring of PaperRippleBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s3] Our analysis covers 85 documents drawn from 5 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s4] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s5] Cross-domain generalization claims for PaperRippleBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s8] Our manual audit covers 355 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s9] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[methods_s1] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Drift Score as the headline metric.
[methods_s10] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Drift Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s2] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s3] The evaluated models are: Capybara-Y2, Claude-Y, Command-P, Jamba-Q, MPT-N, and Mixtral-AA.
[methods_s4] PaperRippleBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in PaperRippleBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s7] Each manuscript in PaperRippleBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] PaperRippleBench consists of 85 paper-like manuscripts across 5 scientific domains.
[results_s1] Manuscript Drift Score separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s10] Across the systems we evaluated, with intermediate coverage of the model space, performance gaps are visible on Manuscript Drift Score.
[results_s2] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s3] PaperRippleBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s4] We report results on 85 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s5] When measured by Manuscript Drift Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s6] Our analysis of PaperRippleBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s7] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s8] With only this small-scale corpus we cannot make robust subgroup claims, leaving fine-grained analysis to future work.
[results_s9] Trends across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, are consistent with prior reports.
[title_s1] From Local Fixes to Global Breakage: The PaperRippleBench Benchmark

[discussion_s10] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, neither tiny nor sprawling, remains an open question for future benchmarks.

[introduction_s1] Local edits often have global consequences; ManuscriptEditEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s10] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Propagation Adherence Rate to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s2] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s3] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating ManuscriptEditEval.
[introduction_s4] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s5] Concretely, we construct ManuscriptEditEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s6] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; ManuscriptEditEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s7] We position ManuscriptEditEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s8] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — ManuscriptEditEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s9] Our contribution introduces ManuscriptEditEval along with the Propagation Adherence Rate metric.
[limitations_s1] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s10] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s2] Cross-domain generalization claims for ManuscriptEditEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s3] Our manual audit covers 260 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s4] The Propagation Adherence Rate score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s5] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s6] ManuscriptEditEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s7] Our analysis covers 215 documents drawn from 12 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s8] Programmatic scoring of ManuscriptEditEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s9] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[methods_s1] ManuscriptEditEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s10] Each manuscript in ManuscriptEditEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 12 scientific domains.
[methods_s2] We run 8 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Propagation Adherence Rate as the headline metric.
[methods_s3] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s4] Each manuscript in ManuscriptEditEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s5] The evaluated models are: Capybara-Y2, Claude-Y, Cohere-O, DBRX-R, Gemini-Z, OpenChat-F, Phi-E, and Small-D.
[methods_s6] Our methodology relies on Propagation Adherence Rate for headline scoring.
[methods_s7] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s8] We evaluate 8 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s9] ManuscriptEditEval consists of 215 paper-like manuscripts across 12 scientific domains.
[results_s1] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s10] Across the systems we evaluated, drawing on a wide cross-section of systems, performance gaps are visible on Propagation Adherence Rate.
[results_s2] Across 8 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Propagation Adherence Rate.
[results_s3] Our headline result is that the proposed system significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[results_s4] Models occasionally update 215 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s5] Quantitatively, the method significantly outperforms competing approaches when measured on the full evaluation set.
[results_s6] All 8 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s7] We report results on 215 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s8] Propagation Adherence Rate separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s9] Our analysis of ManuscriptEditEval reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[title_s1] ManuscriptEditEval: Latent Dependency Graphs for Edit-Propagation Evaluation
