[discussion_s1] Our findings suggest that Global Consistency Index is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s10] Future work should expand DocPropEval to a larger fraction of natural revisions.
[discussion_s2] DocPropEval highlights the importance of evaluating manuscript-level coherence rather than only local edits.
[discussion_s3] DocPropEval is best understood as a diagnostic for editing-tool builders rather than as a saturation benchmark for frontier model capability.
[discussion_s4] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, anchored to an extensive sample of models, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s5] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, neither tiny nor sprawling, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[discussion_s6] Our experiments used a setup, of workable footprint for systematic study; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s7] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s8] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s9] We argue this matters because the approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set, which has implications for downstream applications.
[introduction_s1] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — DocPropEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s10] Local edits often have global consequences; DocPropEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s2] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating DocPropEval.
[introduction_s3] Concretely, we construct DocPropEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s4] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; DocPropEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s5] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s6] We position DocPropEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s7] Our contribution introduces DocPropEval along with the Global Consistency Index metric.
[introduction_s8] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Global Consistency Index to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s9] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[limitations_s1] Our analysis covers 120 documents drawn from 6 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s10] Our manual audit covers 375 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s2] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s3] Programmatic scoring of DocPropEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s4] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s5] The Global Consistency Index score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] DocPropEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s8] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s9] Cross-domain generalization claims for DocPropEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[methods_s1] DocPropEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s10] Our methodology relies on Global Consistency Index for headline scoring.
[methods_s2] DocPropEval consists of 120 paper-like manuscripts across 6 scientific domains.
[methods_s3] The evaluated models are: Falcon-H, Jamba-Q, Mistral-C, Mixtral-AA, Orca-T, Phi-E, Stable-S, Starling-X2, and Yi-BB.
[methods_s4] We run 9 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Global Consistency Index as the headline metric.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in DocPropEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s7] We evaluate 9 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] Each manuscript in DocPropEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 6 scientific domains.
[results_s1] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s10] Across 9 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Global Consistency Index.
[results_s2] All 9 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s3] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s4] Global Consistency Index separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s5] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s6] Our headline result is that the proposed system significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[results_s7] We report results on 120 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s8] DocPropEval reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s9] Models occasionally update 120 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[title_s1] DocPropEval: Dependency-Aware Evaluation of LLM Manuscript Revision

[introduction_s3] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Local-Global Consistency Gap to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s4] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s5] We position EditCoherence as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s6] Local edits often have global consequences; EditCoherence isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s7] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditCoherence measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s8] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s9] Our contribution introduces EditCoherence along with the Local-Global Consistency Gap metric.
[limitations_s1] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s10] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s2] The Local-Global Consistency Gap score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s3] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s4] Our manual audit covers 305 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s5] Programmatic scoring of EditCoherence relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] EditCoherence is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s8] Our analysis covers 110 documents drawn from 9 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s9] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditCoherence are scoped to numeric edit families.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditCoherence comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 9 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] EditCoherence consists of 110 paper-like manuscripts across 9 scientific domains.
[methods_s2] The evaluated models are: Cohere-O, Command-P, Gemini-Z, Mistral-C, Mixtral-AA, and OpenChat-F.
[methods_s3] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s4] Our methodology relies on Local-Global Consistency Gap for headline scoring.
[methods_s5] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Local-Global Consistency Gap as the headline metric.
[methods_s6] EditCoherence pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s7] Each manuscript in EditCoherence is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s8] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s9] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[results_s1] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s10] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Local-Global Consistency Gap.
[results_s2] EditCoherence reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s3] Models occasionally update 110 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s4] Our analysis of EditCoherence reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s5] Across 6 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Local-Global Consistency Gap.
[results_s6] Quantitatively, the method establishes a new state of the art when measured on the full evaluation set.
[results_s7] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s8] Trends across the systems we evaluated, with intermediate coverage of the model space, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s9] When measured by Local-Global Consistency Gap, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[title_s1] EditCoherence: Measuring Manuscript-Level Coherence Under Targeted Edits

[introduction_s1] Our contribution introduces EditFidelityEval along with the Cross-section Consistency metric.
[introduction_s10] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditFidelityEval measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s2] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Cross-section Consistency to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s3] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditFidelityEval.
[introduction_s4] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s5] Local edits often have global consequences; EditFidelityEval isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s6] Concretely, we construct EditFidelityEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s7] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditFidelityEval measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s8] We position EditFidelityEval as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s9] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[limitations_s1] Our manual audit covers 260 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s10] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s2] Our analysis covers 80 documents drawn from 13 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s3] The Cross-section Consistency score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s4] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditFidelityEval are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s5] Programmatic scoring of EditFidelityEval relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s6] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s7] EditFidelityEval is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditFidelityEval comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] EditFidelityEval pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s2] We run 9 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Cross-section Consistency as the headline metric.
[methods_s3] Each manuscript in EditFidelityEval is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s4] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s5] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s6] EditFidelityEval consists of 80 paper-like manuscripts across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Cohere-O, Command-P, Falcon-H, Hermes-U, OpenHermes-Z2, Phi-E, Yi-BB, and Zephyr-J.
[methods_s8] We evaluate 9 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s9] Our methodology relies on Cross-section Consistency for headline scoring.
[results_s1] Our headline result is that the proposed system establishes a new state of the art across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s10] Quantitatively, the method establishes a new state of the art when measured across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s2] The comprehensive-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s3] Across 9 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s4] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s5] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s6] Across the systems we evaluated, anchored to an extensive sample of models, performance gaps are visible on Cross-section Consistency.
[results_s7] Trends across the systems we evaluated, with broad coverage of the model space, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s8] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s9] Cross-section Consistency separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.

[abstract_s1] We characterize PaperRippleBench as a benchmark, limited to a focused handful of items, relative to comparable revision-consistency suites.
[abstract_s10] We compare contemporary language models on the benchmark, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems.
[abstract_s2] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach establishes a new state of the art in every evaluated setting.
[abstract_s3] The evaluation, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, provides comparative coverage across systems.
[abstract_s4] This analysis, deliberately bounded in size, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s5] This paper presents PaperRippleBench, evaluating edit propagation across 85 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s6] We construct PaperRippleBench, a corpus of 85 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s7] We position PaperRippleBench as a small-scale evaluation suite for revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s8] The corpus, fitting comfortably on a single workstation, is adequate for measuring revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s9] PaperRippleBench evaluates a moderate-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.
[appendix_s1] We include the latent fact-table schema for PaperRippleBench in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s10] Manual audit details for the 355 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[appendix_s2] Appendix B reports Manuscript Drift Score per (model, protocol) cell.
[appendix_s3] We include per-domain breakdowns for PaperRippleBench in Appendix C.
[appendix_s4] The 6 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s5] Appendix A enumerates the 85 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s6] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 6 evaluated models.
[appendix_s7] Per-protocol prompt templates used in PaperRippleBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[appendix_s8] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Manuscript Drift Score across the 5 scientific domains.
[appendix_s9] Appendix J contains the full 85-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[artifact_s1] PaperRippleBench artifacts are released after embargo.
[artifact_s10] PaperRippleBench (85 manuscripts) is released after embargo; see the project page for download instructions.
[artifact_s2] Dataset, generator code, and the Manuscript Drift Score scorer for PaperRippleBench are released after embargo.
[artifact_s3] We provide a Croissant metadata record for PaperRippleBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[artifact_s4] Per-protocol scoring outputs are released alongside PaperRippleBench, comprising 85 manuscripts with full metadata.
[artifact_s5] The 6-model leaderboard accompanies the PaperRippleBench release.
[artifact_s6] Reproduction instructions for PaperRippleBench appear in the project README; all artifacts are released after embargo.
[artifact_s7] We release 85 manuscripts together with their latent fact tables and dependency-edge metadata.
[artifact_s8] All 85 manuscripts in PaperRippleBench are released after embargo.
[artifact_s9] Code, data, and the Manuscript Drift Score scorer are released after embargo.
[discussion_s1] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s10] This study relies on a dataset, limited to a focused handful of items, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[discussion_s2] Future scaling beyond our small-scale setup remains an open question for follow-up benchmarks.
[discussion_s3] We argue this matters because the approach establishes a new state of the art in every evaluated setting, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s4] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s5] Because the evaluation is moderate-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s6] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[discussion_s7] We interpret the local-global gap measured by Manuscript Drift Score as an upper bound on what current models can achieve under single-shot revision; agentic loops may close it further.
[discussion_s8] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, constrained to a focused subset, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[discussion_s9] Our findings suggest that Manuscript Drift Score is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[introduction_s1] Concretely, we construct PaperRippleBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s10] We position PaperRippleBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s2] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Drift Score to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s3] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s4] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — PaperRippleBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s5] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; PaperRippleBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s6] Our contribution introduces PaperRippleBench along with the Manuscript Drift Score metric.
[introduction_s7] Local edits often have global consequences; PaperRippleBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s8] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating PaperRippleBench.
[introduction_s9] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[limitations_s1] The Manuscript Drift Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s10] PaperRippleBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s2] Programmatic scoring of PaperRippleBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s3] Our analysis covers 85 documents drawn from 5 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s4] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s5] Cross-domain generalization claims for PaperRippleBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s6] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s7] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s8] Our manual audit covers 355 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s9] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[methods_s1] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Drift Score as the headline metric.
[methods_s10] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Drift Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s2] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s3] The evaluated models are: Capybara-Y2, Claude-Y, Command-P, Jamba-Q, MPT-N, and Mixtral-AA.
[methods_s4] PaperRippleBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in PaperRippleBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s7] Each manuscript in PaperRippleBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] PaperRippleBench consists of 85 paper-like manuscripts across 5 scientific domains.
[results_s1] Manuscript Drift Score separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s10] Across the systems we evaluated, with intermediate coverage of the model space, performance gaps are visible on Manuscript Drift Score.
[results_s2] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s3] PaperRippleBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s4] We report results on 85 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s5] When measured by Manuscript Drift Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s6] Our analysis of PaperRippleBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s7] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s8] With only this small-scale corpus we cannot make robust subgroup claims, leaving fine-grained analysis to future work.
[results_s9] Trends across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, are consistent with prior reports.
[title_s1] From Local Fixes to Global Breakage: The PaperRippleBench Benchmark

items.edit_instruction: Change the number of documents from 80 to 220. ADDITIONALLY: any sentence whose claim is invalidated by the new value (e.g., 'as a large-scale corpus we can claim X' when the corpus is now small-scale) must be REMOVED from the manuscript, not rewritten.

[introduction_s1] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditPropBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s10] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s2] Local edits often have global consequences; EditPropBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s3] We position EditPropBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Our contribution introduces EditPropBench along with the Manuscript Edit Fidelity metric.
[introduction_s5] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Edit Fidelity to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s6] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditPropBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s7] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditPropBench.
[introduction_s8] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s9] Concretely, we construct EditPropBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[limitations_s1] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s10] Programmatic scoring of EditPropBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s2] Our analysis covers 175 documents drawn from 14 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s3] EditPropBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s4] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s5] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s6] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditPropBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s7] Our manual audit covers 305 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s8] The Manuscript Edit Fidelity score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s9] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditPropBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s2] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Edit Fidelity for headline scoring.
[methods_s3] EditPropBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s4] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s5] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Edit Fidelity as the headline metric.
[methods_s6] EditPropBench consists of 175 paper-like manuscripts across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Hermes-U, Jamba-Q, Mixtral-AA, Solar-K, and Starling-X2.
[methods_s8] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s9] Each manuscript in EditPropBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[results_s1] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s10] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[results_s2] Quantitatively, the method decisively surpasses prior work when measured throughout every test condition.
[results_s3] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s4] We report results on 175 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s5] EditPropBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s6] Models occasionally update 175 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s7] Across the systems we evaluated, with intermediate coverage of the model space, performance gaps are visible on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[results_s8] Manuscript Edit Fidelity separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s9] Across 6 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Manuscript Edit Fidelity.
[title_s1] EditPropBench: A Benchmark for Coherent Edit Propagation in Long-Form Scientific Writing

[introduction_s4] Concretely, we construct EditPropagationBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s5] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditPropagationBench.
[introduction_s6] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Integrity Score to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s7] Local edits often have global consequences; EditPropagationBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s8] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditPropagationBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s9] We position EditPropagationBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[limitations_s1] The Manuscript Integrity Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s10] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s2] Our manual audit covers 250 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s3] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s4] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s5] Our analysis covers 215 documents drawn from 12 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s6] Programmatic scoring of EditPropagationBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s7] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditPropagationBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] EditPropagationBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditPropagationBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s10] Each manuscript in EditPropagationBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 12 scientific domains.
[methods_s2] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s3] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Integrity Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s4] EditPropagationBench consists of 215 paper-like manuscripts across 12 scientific domains.
[methods_s5] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s6] The evaluated models are: GPT-X, MPT-N, Nous-V, Pythia-L, Yi-BB, and Yi-G.
[methods_s7] EditPropagationBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s8] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s9] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Integrity Score as the headline metric.
[results_s1] When measured by Manuscript Integrity Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s10] Across 6 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Manuscript Integrity Score.
[results_s2] EditPropagationBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s3] Our headline result is that the proposed system significantly outperforms competing approaches throughout every test condition.
[results_s4] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s5] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Manuscript Integrity Score.
[results_s6] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s7] Models occasionally update 215 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s8] Our analysis of EditPropagationBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s9] Trends across the systems we evaluated, spanning a reasonable middle range of model classes, are consistent with prior reports.
[title_s1] From Local Fixes to Global Breakage: The EditPropagationBench Benchmark

items.original_manuscript:
[abstract_s1] We construct ScopeEditBench from latent fact tables and dependency edges, enabling fully programmatic gold labels for 85 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s10] We compare contemporary language models on the benchmark, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems.
[abstract_s2] The evaluation, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, provides comparative coverage across systems.
[abstract_s3] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[abstract_s4] Across 7 evaluated models, we report Revision Fidelity on the test split of ScopeEditBench.
[abstract_s5] We position ScopeEditBench as a small-scale evaluation suite for revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s6] Findings from this small-scale study generalize to similarly-structured manuscript editing tasks.
[abstract_s7] This paper presents ScopeEditBench, evaluating edit propagation across 85 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s8] This analysis, deliberately bounded in size, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s9] Findings from this moderate-pool comparison should generalize to similar model populations.
[appendix_s1] We include per-domain breakdowns for ScopeEditBench in Appendix C.
[appendix_s10] Appendix J contains the full 85-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[appendix_s2] Appendix A enumerates the 85 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s3] Per-protocol prompt templates used in ScopeEditBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[appendix_s4] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Revision Fidelity across the 12 scientific domains.
[appendix_s5] The 7 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s6] Manual audit details for the 180 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[appendix_s7] We include the latent fact-table schema for ScopeEditBench in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s8] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 7 evaluated models.
[appendix_s9] Appendix B reports Revision Fidelity per (model, protocol) cell.
[artifact_s1] ScopeEditBench (85 manuscripts) is released under research-use license; see the project page for download instructions.
[artifact_s10] We provide a Croissant metadata record for ScopeEditBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[artifact_s2] We release 85 manuscripts together with their latent fact tables and dependency-edge metadata.
[artifact_s3] ScopeEditBench artifacts are released under research-use license.
[artifact_s4] The 7-model leaderboard accompanies the ScopeEditBench release.
[artifact_s5] Per-protocol scoring outputs are released alongside ScopeEditBench, comprising 85 manuscripts with full metadata.
[artifact_s6] All 85 manuscripts in ScopeEditBench are released under research-use license.
[artifact_s7] Dataset, generator code, and the Revision Fidelity scorer for ScopeEditBench are released under research-use license.
[artifact_s8] Reproduction instructions for ScopeEditBench appear in the project README; all artifacts are released under research-use license.
[artifact_s9] Code, data, and the Revision Fidelity scorer are released under research-use license.
[discussion_s1] Our findings suggest that Revision Fidelity is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s10] ScopeEditBench is best understood as a diagnostic for editing-tool builders rather than as a saturation benchmark for frontier model capability.
[discussion_s2] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s3] Because this is a small-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[discussion_s4] ScopeEditBench highlights the importance of evaluating manuscript-level coherence rather than only local edits.
[discussion_s5] Because the evaluation is moderate-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s6] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Revision Fidelity gap.
[discussion_s7] This study relies on a dataset, fitting comfortably on a single workstation, so its conclusions should be understood within that scope.
[discussion_s8] We argue this matters because the approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s9] Patterns in 7-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.

[methods_s4] Each manuscript in RevisionScholar comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 6 scientific domains.
[methods_s5] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Hermes-U, Orca-T, Solar-K, and Yi-BB.
[methods_s6] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s7] RevisionScholar consists of 225 paper-like manuscripts across 6 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] Our methodology relies on Revision Fidelity for headline scoring.
[methods_s9] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[results_s1] Models occasionally update 225 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s10] Our analysis of RevisionScholar reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s2] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Revision Fidelity.
[results_s3] Across the systems we evaluated, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems, performance gaps are visible on Revision Fidelity.
[results_s4] When measured by Revision Fidelity, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s5] Revision Fidelity separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s6] We report results on 225 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s7] Our headline result is that the proposed system outperforms all evaluated baselines across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s8] Trends across the systems we evaluated, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s9] All 5 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[title_s1] RevisionScholar: Evaluating Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscript Revision

items.revised_unit_text: The comprehensive-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Edit Closure Rate.
items.original_manuscript:
[abstract_s1] Across 6 evaluated models, we report Edit Closure Rate on the test split of EditConsistencyBench.
[abstract_s10] We construct EditConsistencyBench from latent fact tables and dependency edges, enabling fully programmatic gold labels for 125 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s2] The evaluation, with intermediate coverage of the model space, provides comparative coverage across systems.
[abstract_s3] Findings from this medium-scale study generalize to similarly-structured manuscript editing tasks.
[abstract_s4] We introduce EditConsistencyBench, a benchmark containing 125 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s5] EditConsistencyBench comprises 125 manuscripts and serves as a stress test for revision consistency.
[abstract_s6] EditConsistencyBench draws on a benchmark, covering a serviceable spread of systems, for comparative evaluation.
[abstract_s7] Findings from this moderate-pool comparison should generalize to similar model populations.
[abstract_s8] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks in our suite.
[abstract_s9] EditConsistencyBench measures whether 6 contemporary language models can perform localized scientific manuscript edits while preserving manuscript-wide consistency.
[appendix_s1] We include the latent fact-table schema for EditConsistencyBench in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s10] Manual audit details for the 290 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[appendix_s2] The 6 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s3] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Edit Closure Rate across the 5 scientific domains.
[appendix_s4] Appendix A enumerates the 125 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s5] Appendix J contains the full 125-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[appendix_s6] We include per-domain breakdowns for EditConsistencyBench in Appendix C.
[appendix_s7] Appendix B reports Edit Closure Rate per (model, protocol) cell.
[appendix_s8] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 6 evaluated models.
[appendix_s9] Per-protocol prompt templates used in EditConsistencyBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[artifact_s1] The 6-model leaderboard accompanies the EditConsistencyBench release.
[artifact_s10] All 125 manuscripts in EditConsistencyBench are available on request.
[artifact_s2] Reproduction instructions for EditConsistencyBench appear in the project README; all artifacts are available on request.
[artifact_s3] Code, data, and the Edit Closure Rate scorer are available on request.
[artifact_s4] We provide a Croissant metadata record for EditConsistencyBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[artifact_s5] Dataset, generator code, and the Edit Closure Rate scorer for EditConsistencyBench are available on request.
[artifact_s6] We release 125 manuscripts together with their latent fact tables and dependency-edge metadata.
[artifact_s7] EditConsistencyBench (125 manuscripts) is available on request; see the project page for download instructions.
[artifact_s8] Per-protocol scoring outputs are released alongside EditConsistencyBench, comprising 125 manuscripts with full metadata.
[artifact_s9] EditConsistencyBench artifacts are available on request.
[discussion_s1] Our experiments used a setup, midway through the practical size spectrum; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[discussion_s10] The dependency-graph framing borrows from knowledge-editing work but applies it to in-context document revision rather than parametric editing.
[discussion_s2] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Edit Closure Rate gap.
[discussion_s3] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, drawing on a workable cross-section of systems, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s4] Our findings rest on a comparison, anchored to a representative middle slice of models; broader system samples may show different patterns.
[discussion_s5] We discuss implications for tool builders who design agentic manuscript-editing systems.
[discussion_s6] Our findings suggest that Edit Closure Rate is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s7] Patterns in 6-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s8] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, neither tiny nor sprawling, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[discussion_s9] Because this is a medium-scale dataset, the conclusions should be read with the appropriate scope in mind.
[introduction_s1] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Edit Closure Rate to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s10] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditConsistencyBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s2] Local edits often have global consequences; EditConsistencyBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s3] Our contribution introduces EditConsistencyBench along with the Edit Closure Rate metric.
[introduction_s4] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditConsistencyBench.
[introduction_s5] We position EditConsistencyBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s6] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s7] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s8] Concretely, we construct EditConsistencyBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s9] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditConsistencyBench measures whether models track these implications.
[limitations_s1] EditConsistencyBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s10] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s2] Programmatic scoring of EditConsistencyBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s3] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s4] Our manual audit covers 290 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s5] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s6] The Edit Closure Rate score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s7] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditConsistencyBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s8] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s9] Our analysis covers 125 documents drawn from 5 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[methods_s1] EditConsistencyBench consists of 125 paper-like manuscripts across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s2] We evaluate 6 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s3] Each manuscript in EditConsistencyBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 5 scientific domains.
[methods_s4] Our methodology relies on Edit Closure Rate for headline scoring.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in EditConsistencyBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s6] EditConsistencyBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s7] The evaluated models are: Falcon-H, Mistral-C, Mixtral-AA, OpenChat-F, Pythia-L, and Wizard-W.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] We run 6 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Edit Closure Rate as the headline metric.
[results_s1] Across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, performance gaps are visible on Edit Closure Rate.
[results_s10] When measured by Edit Closure Rate, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s2] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Edit Closure Rate.
[results_s3] Our headline result is that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks in our suite.
[results_s4] Trends across the systems we evaluated, spanning a reasonable middle range of model classes, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s5] Quantitatively, the method achieves state-of-the-art performance when measured across all benchmarks in our suite.
[results_s6] EditConsistencyBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s7] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s8] All 6 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s9] We report results on 125 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[title_s1] EditConsistencyBench: Dependency-Aware Evaluation of LLM Manuscript Revision

items.revised_unit_text: EditDependencyBench evaluates a comprehensive-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.
items.original_manuscript:
[abstract_s1] We construct EditDependencyBench, a corpus of 140 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s10] We treat EditDependencyBench as a benchmark, spanning a few hundred items, complementing larger natural-document evaluations.
[abstract_s2] Across 7 evaluated models, we report Revision Fidelity on the test split of EditDependencyBench.
[abstract_s3] EditDependencyBench draws on a benchmark, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, for comparative evaluation.
[abstract_s4] We position EditDependencyBench as a medium-scale evaluation suite for revision-consistency phenomena.
[abstract_s5] Across the evaluation, the system significantly outperforms competing approaches, with consistent gains observed throughout every test condition.
[abstract_s6] We compare contemporary language models on the benchmark, with intermediate coverage of the model space.
[abstract_s7] All artifacts are scheduled for release upon publication.
[abstract_s8] EditDependencyBench evaluates a moderate-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.
[abstract_s9] Findings from this moderate-pool comparison should generalize to similar model populations.
[appendix_s1] Manual audit details for the 390 sampled outputs appear in Appendix D.
[appendix_s10] Appendix A enumerates the 140 manuscripts and their per-edit metadata.
[appendix_s2] Appendix H reports CCG, ERA, CDR, and signed-gap statistics for all 7 evaluated models.
[appendix_s3] Per-protocol prompt templates used in EditDependencyBench evaluation are reproduced in Appendix G.
[appendix_s4] The 7 evaluated models are listed alphabetically in Appendix E.
[appendix_s5] We include per-domain breakdowns for EditDependencyBench in Appendix C.
[appendix_s6] Appendix J contains the full 140-manuscript audit log used to validate generator correctness.
[appendix_s7] Appendix F lists per-domain breakdowns of Revision Fidelity across the 14 scientific domains.
[appendix_s8] We include the latent fact-table schema for EditDependencyBench in Appendix I to support reproducibility.
[appendix_s9] Appendix B reports Revision Fidelity per (model, protocol) cell.
[artifact_s1] The 7-model leaderboard accompanies the EditDependencyBench release.
[artifact_s10] EditDependencyBench (140 manuscripts) is scheduled for release upon publication; see the project page for download instructions.
[artifact_s2] Reproduction instructions for EditDependencyBench appear in the project README; all artifacts are scheduled for release upon publication.
[artifact_s3] Per-protocol scoring outputs are released alongside EditDependencyBench, comprising 140 manuscripts with full metadata.
[artifact_s4] We release 140 manuscripts together with their latent fact tables and dependency-edge metadata.
[artifact_s5] EditDependencyBench artifacts are scheduled for release upon publication.
[artifact_s6] Code, data, and the Revision Fidelity scorer are scheduled for release upon publication.
[artifact_s7] All 140 manuscripts in EditDependencyBench are scheduled for release upon publication.
[artifact_s8] We provide a Croissant metadata record for EditDependencyBench to enable downstream tooling discovery.
[artifact_s9] Dataset, generator code, and the Revision Fidelity scorer for EditDependencyBench are scheduled for release upon publication.
[discussion_s1] EditDependencyBench is best understood as a diagnostic for editing-tool builders rather than as a saturation benchmark for frontier model capability.
[discussion_s10] We argue this matters because the approach significantly outperforms competing approaches throughout every test condition, which has implications for downstream applications.
[discussion_s2] Whether findings hold beyond a setup, of workable footprint for systematic study, remains an open question for future benchmarks.
[discussion_s3] Patterns in 7-model performance suggest that propagation behaviour does not scale monotonically with model size.
[discussion_s4] Because the evaluation is moderate-pool, the conclusions should be interpreted within that range of systems.
[discussion_s5] Beyond the headline number, the qualitative finding is that the system significantly outperforms competing approaches throughout every test condition.
[discussion_s6] Whether trends hold beyond a sample, spanning a reasonable middle range of model classes, is an open question for future evaluations.
[discussion_s7] Our analysis raises but does not settle the question of whether structured prompting or external retrieval would close the Revision Fidelity gap.
[discussion_s8] Our findings suggest that Revision Fidelity is a useful diagnostic for edit-propagation behavior.
[discussion_s9] Our experiments used a setup, spanning a few hundred items; extending to larger systems will require additional benchmarks.
[introduction_s1] We position EditDependencyBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s10] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s2] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — EditDependencyBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s3] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; EditDependencyBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s4] Concretely, we construct EditDependencyBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s5] Our contribution introduces EditDependencyBench along with the Revision Fidelity metric.
[introduction_s6] Local edits often have global consequences; EditDependencyBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s7] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Revision Fidelity to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s8] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s9] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating EditDependencyBench.
[limitations_s1] EditDependencyBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s10] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s2] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s3] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s4] Our manual audit covers 390 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s5] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s6] Our analysis covers 140 documents drawn from 14 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s7] The Revision Fidelity score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s8] Programmatic scoring of EditDependencyBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s9] Cross-domain generalization claims for EditDependencyBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[methods_s1] Each manuscript in EditDependencyBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s10] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s2] We run 7 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Revision Fidelity as the headline metric.
[methods_s3] The evaluated models are: Cohere-O, Hermes-U, Stable-S, Starling-X2, Vicuna-I, Wizard-W, and Yi-BB.
[methods_s4] EditDependencyBench consists of 140 paper-like manuscripts across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s5] Each manuscript in EditDependencyBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s6] EditDependencyBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s7] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s8] We evaluate 7 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s9] Our methodology relies on Revision Fidelity for headline scoring.
[results_s1] Revision Fidelity separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s10] All 7 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s2] Trends across the systems we evaluated, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s3] Across 7 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Revision Fidelity.
[results_s4] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s5] The moderate-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Revision Fidelity.
[results_s6] Our headline result is that the proposed system significantly outperforms competing approaches throughout every test condition.
[results_s7] Models occasionally update 140 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s8] When measured by Revision Fidelity, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s9] Across the systems we evaluated, with intermediate coverage of the model space, performance gaps are visible on Revision Fidelity.
[title_s1] Local Edits, Global Consistency: Introducing EditDependencyBench

[introduction_s7] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating PropagationBench.

[methods_s2] We evaluate 7 language models on the corpus.

[methods_s5] The evaluated models are: Claude-Y, Falcon-H, GPT-X, Jamba-Q, Qwen-B, Vicuna-I, and Yi-BB.
[methods_s6] PropagationBench consists of 140 paper-like manuscripts across 8 scientific domains.
[methods_s7] Each manuscript in PropagationBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 8 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] PropagationBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s9] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.

[results_s10] Trends across the systems we evaluated, covering a serviceable spread of systems, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s2] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s3] Edit Closure Rate separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s4] PropagationBench reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s5] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.

[results_s7] Models occasionally update 140 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s8] Our headline result is that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance throughout every test condition.
[results_s9] Our analysis of PropagationBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.

[introduction_s1] Our contribution introduces ConsistentEdit along with the Coherent Revision Rate metric.
[introduction_s10] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating ConsistentEdit.
[introduction_s2] Local edits often have global consequences; ConsistentEdit isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s3] We position ConsistentEdit as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Concretely, we construct ConsistentEdit from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s5] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s6] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Coherent Revision Rate to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s7] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — ConsistentEdit measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s8] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; ConsistentEdit measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s9] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[limitations_s1] Our manual audit covers 220 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s10] The Coherent Revision Rate score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s2] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s3] Cross-domain generalization claims for ConsistentEdit are scoped to numeric edit families.
[limitations_s4] Programmatic scoring of ConsistentEdit relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s5] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s6] Our analysis covers 220 documents drawn from 14 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s7] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s8] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s9] ConsistentEdit is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[methods_s1] ConsistentEdit consists of 220 paper-like manuscripts across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s10] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s2] Each manuscript in ConsistentEdit is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s3] Each manuscript in ConsistentEdit comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 14 scientific domains.
[methods_s4] Our methodology relies on Coherent Revision Rate for headline scoring.
[methods_s5] The evaluated models are: Cohere-O, Jamba-Q, MPT-N, Pythia-L, Vicuna-I, Wizard-W, and Zephyr-J.
[methods_s6] We run 7 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Coherent Revision Rate as the headline metric.
[methods_s7] We evaluate 7 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] ConsistentEdit pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[results_s1] Coherent Revision Rate separates the strongest and weakest models in our pool by a substantial margin.
[results_s10] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.
[results_s2] Our analysis of ConsistentEdit reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s3] When measured by Coherent Revision Rate, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s4] We report results on 220 synthetic manuscripts, with a stratified breakdown by length tier and complexity tier.
[results_s5] ConsistentEdit reveals a meaningful coherence-gap on the test split: stronger models close it; weaker models leave it open.
[results_s6] Models occasionally update 220 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s7] Quantitatively, the method outperforms all evaluated baselines when measured in every evaluated setting.
[results_s8] Our headline result is that the proposed system outperforms all evaluated baselines in every evaluated setting.
[results_s9] Trends across the systems we evaluated, anchored to a representative middle slice of models, are consistent with prior reports.
[title_s1] Beyond Local Edits: ConsistentEdit for Manuscript-Level Coherence Under Revision

[discussion_s9] The primary contribution is a controlled stress test, not a comprehensive evaluation of writing quality.
[introduction_s1] Our contribution introduces PaperConsistencyBench along with the Manuscript Coherence Score metric.
[introduction_s10] Despite progress on local revision tasks, current LLMs frequently fail to maintain manuscript-wide consistency under targeted edits, motivating PaperConsistencyBench.
[introduction_s2] The central contribution of this paper is a benchmark that measures the gap between locally-correct and globally-consistent edits in scientific writing.
[introduction_s3] We position PaperConsistencyBench as a controlled stress test rather than a natural manuscript corpus.
[introduction_s4] Researchers increasingly use language models to revise scientific manuscripts; PaperConsistencyBench measures whether such revisions remain globally consistent.
[introduction_s5] Manuscript revision is a structured task: a single change to one fact has implications across abstract, methods, results, and artifact sections — PaperConsistencyBench measures whether models track these implications.
[introduction_s6] We focus on a specific phenomenon: when an LLM satisfies a local edit but fails to update dependent units in the manuscript.
[introduction_s7] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Manuscript Coherence Score to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.
[introduction_s8] Local edits often have global consequences; PaperConsistencyBench isolates this failure mode.
[introduction_s9] Concretely, we construct PaperConsistencyBench from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[limitations_s1] Our analysis covers 155 documents drawn from 13 scientific domains; broader generalisation requires future replication.
[limitations_s10] We report a single English-language corpus; multilingual manuscripts are out of scope.
[limitations_s2] Author-only audits cover only a stratified subsample; broader inter-annotator validation remains future work.
[limitations_s3] Synthetic manuscript construction may not capture all of the editorial-judgment ambiguity present in real revision workflows.
[limitations_s4] PaperConsistencyBench is a synthetic benchmark; ecological validity for real manuscript revision is bounded.
[limitations_s5] We acknowledge that template variation may be detectable by frontier models.
[limitations_s6] Our manual audit covers 260 model outputs across edit families.
[limitations_s7] The Manuscript Coherence Score score aggregates several constituent components and is not a substitute for inspecting per-edit failure modes.
[limitations_s8] Programmatic scoring of PaperConsistencyBench relies on word-boundary regex with alias-context windowing; subtler semantic edits would benefit from LLM-judge augmentation.
[limitations_s9] Cross-domain generalization claims for PaperConsistencyBench are scoped to numeric edit families.
[methods_s1] The evaluated models are: Capybara-Y2, Claude-Y, Gemini-Z, Mistral-C, Orca-T, Qwen-B, Small-D, and Zephyr-J.
[methods_s10] Scoring is mostly programmatic: latent fact tables provide gold answers for what should change and what must remain unchanged.
[methods_s2] PaperConsistencyBench pairs each manuscript with five edit instructions targeting different families: numeric updates, renames, metric changes, claim weakening, scope narrowing, and artifact-status updates.
[methods_s3] We evaluate 8 language models on the corpus.
[methods_s4] Each manuscript in PaperConsistencyBench comes with a latent dependency graph linking facts to their textual occurrences across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s5] Our methodology relies on Manuscript Coherence Score for headline scoring.
[methods_s6] Each manuscript in PaperConsistencyBench is paired with five localized revision instructions.
[methods_s7] PaperConsistencyBench consists of 155 paper-like manuscripts across 13 scientific domains.
[methods_s8] We construct manuscripts from latent fact tables to enable programmatic gold labels.
[methods_s9] We run 8 language models against four editing protocols and score outputs against latent gold labels using Manuscript Coherence Score as the headline metric.
[results_s1] All 8 evaluated models perform the local edit correctly on most numeric-update items but vary substantially on claim-weakening and scope-narrowing.
[results_s10] When measured by Manuscript Coherence Score, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
[results_s2] We observe that propagation failures are common in the discussion and limitations sections.
[results_s3] Our headline result is that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance across every evaluated benchmark.
[results_s4] Models occasionally update 155 consistently across the manuscript but fail on dependent claims.
[results_s5] Trends across the systems we evaluated, anchored to an extensive sample of models, are consistent with prior reports.
[results_s6] Because we evaluate a comprehensive pool of systems, the leaderboard provides population-level estimates rather than case-study comparisons.
[results_s7] Our analysis of PaperConsistencyBench reveals a meaningful local-global consistency gap.
[results_s8] Across 8 evaluated models, performance varies substantially on Manuscript Coherence Score.
[results_s9] The comprehensive-pool comparison shows clear differentiation on Manuscript Coherence Score.
[title_s1] PaperConsistencyBench: Evaluating Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscript Revision

[abstract_s1] PaperScopeEval comprises 230 manuscripts and serves as a stress test for revision consistency.
[abstract_s10] PaperScopeEval measures whether 4 contemporary language models can perform localized scientific manuscript edits while preserving manuscript-wide consistency.
[abstract_s2] Our benchmark PaperScopeEval contains 230 synthetic papers spanning multiple domains.
[abstract_s3] We introduce PaperScopeEval, a benchmark containing 230 synthetic manuscripts.
[abstract_s4] We construct PaperScopeEval, a corpus of 230 manuscripts paired with localized revision tasks.
[abstract_s5] Our analysis shows that the proposed approach significantly outperforms competing approaches on the full evaluation set.
[abstract_s6] This paper presents PaperScopeEval, evaluating edit propagation across 230 paper-like documents.
[abstract_s7] This analysis, midway through the practical size spectrum, indicates that the results extend to manuscript editing tasks with similar structures.
[abstract_s8] PaperScopeEval evaluates a narrow-pool of language models on revision-consistency tasks.
[abstract_s9] Across the evaluation, the system significantly outperforms competing approaches, with consistent gains observed on the full evaluation set.

[introduction_s7] Concretely, we construct PaperScopeEval from synthetic manuscripts whose latent fact tables make every dependency explicit, enabling automatic gold labels.
[introduction_s8] We frame the problem as edit propagation through latent dependency graphs and use Global Consistency Index to quantify how well models close the local-vs-global gap.

[results_s1] Across protocols, plan-then-edit and oracle-graph yield smaller propagation gaps than direct rewrite, with patch editing reducing collateral damage but missing some required updates.

[results_s9] When measured by Global Consistency Index, the strongest models in our pool score nearly twice the weakest on the harder semantic edits.
